name: Four Kings
description: Last year, 13 months after it opened, Four Kings debuted in the No. 2 spot on our list. Mike Long and Franky Ho’s electric Cantonese-by-way-of-their-fevered-brains cooking and the energy of their Chinatown restaurant had us in a chokehold. Located underneath what is surely the most delicious smelling Kumon in the city, it was exactly the type of feel-good party San Francisco needed in 2025.

name: The Progress
description: A solo diner in ripped jeans is sitting at the bar, eating an off-menu hot dog topped with kimchi. Behind him, members of a wedding party in tuxedos and gowns sling their arms around one another’s shoulders as they ascend the stairs to the private dining room. The Progress, statuesque sister to State Bird Provisions (No. 36) and Anchovy Bar, is a restaurant for all occasions, the kind of place where you can picture celebrating life’s milestones and Saturday’s late-night cravings alike.

name: Burdell
description: Geoff Davis honors the agrarian roots of soul food at Burdell, which is named after his grandmother. The interior of the Oakland restaurant appears as if in a perpetual state of golden hour, with copper-tinted glass panes, cream-colored walls and appreciation for all things vintage: a 1970s sound system, furniture like repurposed church pews and modest dishware with halos of flowers. Unfussy plating puts the spotlight on the brazenly flavorful cooking.

name: Copra
description: Chef Srijith Gopinathan’s restaurants on the Peninsula (including No. 70, Eylan) are firmly Cal-Indian, with local persimmons in the chaat and tomatillos in the butter chicken sauce. Not so with Copra, his San Francisco restaurant. Why? He was tired of commuting to the South Bay to find the food he grew up with in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, so he built the restaurant he wanted to see in the world.

name: Sun Moon Studio
description: When Sun Moon Studio landed near the tippy top of this list last year, it was already a difficult restaurant to book. With just 12 seats in the dining room and four hands in the kitchen, it’s an operation that is modest in size, if not ambition. Sarah Cooper handles all the baking, Alan Hsu oversees the cooking, and where their talents intertwine — a roe-topped egg custard tart in the flakiest shell, a mini lap cheong hot dog on a steamed brioche bun — dazzling things occur.

name: Popoca
description: Popoca is California’s crown jewel for Salvadoran cooking, not only for its high-caliber pupusas but for the way it widens diners’ perspectives of the cuisine. Snack like a Salvi and order craggy fried chicken wings drizzled with chile-laced syrup or the “guaca-mol,” mashed avocados garnished with smoked egg yolk and accompanied by tostadas brushed with rendered beef fat.

name: Breadbelly
description: Since 2018, Breadbelly has quietly slung superlative sandwiches and cult-status pastries out of a minuscule storefront in the Richmond District. But last summer brought major changes: The Asian American bakery opened a second location inside an enormous former shipbuilding facility — big enough to house indoor pickleball courts — at Pier 70.

name: Rich Table
description: For nearly 14 years, Rich Table has been a paragon of hospitality and a stalwart of thrilling, intrinsically delicious cooking — a formula for staying perpetually booked. The restaurant’s defining dishes continue to stun, from crackly doughnuts powdered in porcini mushrooms to pasta coated in luxurious uni butter.

name: SingleThread
description: At tasting menu restaurants, it is customary to begin with a parade of amuse-bouches, wee little bites that prime you for an evening of — one hopes — surprise and delight. If this procession is normally akin to a Fourth of July celebration, the opening of a meal at SingleThread is an extravaganza worthy of the Platinum Jubilee.

name: Fù Huì Huá
description: Last year, William Ge and his son Tao Ge, both from Shanghai, opened Fù Huì Huá out of frustration that high-end Chinese cuisine lacked representation in the United States. Initially, non-Asian diners at their eight-seat counter expressed some confusion. “Why are you speaking Chinese?” one asked. “Isn’t this a Japanese restaurant?” Another, when presented with a serving of clay pot rice, gleefully professed their love for Korean bibimbap.

name: Taishan Cuisine
description: After last year’s Top 100 gala, where did the chef honorees go to celebrate? Taishan Cuisine, of course. Open every day until 3 a.m., the no-frills Chinatown restaurant draws a motley crew of international Chinese students, solo diners deep in their cups and hospitality workers in need of a late-night meal.

name: Jules
description: After five years of frenzied experimentation with regional styles, the Bay Area pizza scene has yielded a definitive cross-pollinated breed that mixes East Coast technique with West Coast sensibility. Jules is the brightest star of this emergent tradition: Chef-owner Max Blachman-Gentile, a Tartine Bakery vet, uses Bay Area sourdough to produce crispy New Haven-style pizzas with bubbly, charred crusts that have a distinctive exterior crackle and incredibly chewy interior.

name: Soba Ichi
description: Soba Ichi chef Koichi Ishii is a virtuoso in the art of soba: beautifully supple buckwheat strings, which he makes from scratch. Diners line up six days a week to soothe their souls with soba noodle soups made with a dashi-based broth brightened by hints of yuzu.

name: Joodooboo
description: Delicate. Subtle. Gentle. These are the words that tend to orbit discussions of Steve Joo’s cooking. Debuting in 2022, Joodooboo has transcended its humble origins as a banchan shop for freshly made dooboo (tofu) to become a singular expression of the softer side of Korean cooking.

name: True Laurel
description: Everyone knows True Laurel is a world-class bar. What everyone may not know is that True Laurel is also a superlative restaurant, worth visiting even if you only stick to water (but don’t do that).

name: Friends Only
description: There’s no shortage of omakase restaurants in San Francisco, but only one made this list. Friends Only stands apart thanks to its focus on dry-aging, a technique that many sushi restaurants employ, but few with the same amount of zeal. When you enter the restaurant, you’ll see a refrigerator dedicated to the purpose — like Dr. Caligari’s cabinet, but with suspended fish instead of somnambulists.

name: Benu
description: When a restaurant’s calling card is as memorable as the one at Benu — a too-pretty to eat marinated mussel, whose insides are threaded with glass noodles and colorful bands of vegetables — there’s no need to change it. It’s a prelude to a meal marked by the obsessive, tweezered exactitude one would expect from an establishment that was San Francisco’s first restaurant to attain three Michelin stars, which it has held for more than a decade

name: Quince
description: The Tusks are on a roll. Their wine bar, Verjus, reopened a year and a half ago, and Bar Coto, their Milanese all-day café, is slated to debut later this year. But Quince, which underwent a deep-plane facelift in 2023, remains the center of their restaurant universe, a fine dining extravaganza that cocoons diners in both immaculate service and velvet upholstery in shades of fawn and sage.

name: Tacos Oscar
description: The Bay Area has countless taquerias, but only one Tacos Oscar. The menu features neither carne asada nor super burritos. Instead, it offers tacos made with fluffy handmade tortillas and topped with things like crunchy, charred broccoli.

name: San Ho Won
description: San Francisco does not have a deep bench of Korean barbecue restaurants — for that, you have to head down to the South Bay — but what we do have is San Ho Won, Corey Lee and Jeong-In Hwang’s temple to all things grilled.

name: Sons & Daughters
description: Acidity is held in the highest regard at Sons & Daughters. Its deft use of house-made vinegars, of which there are many, accords luminosity throughout the marathon tasting menu. The restaurant recently moved to a new space in the Mission District, where the dining room is enveloped in wall-to-ceiling reclaimed Douglas fir and redwood like a boutique sauna, and the kitchen is four times larger than its former Nob Hill home.

name: Mägo
description: Mägo is not only one of the best tasting menu deals in the Bay Area, it’s one of the best tasting menus, period. Mark Liberman’s eight-course tour of South America is $90 on weekdays ($125 on Friday and Saturdays). But there’s no skimping on details, from the mushroom arepa made with two types of masa to the nixtamalized fruit as a final bite.

name: Sandy’s
description: What festive ooh-and-ahh item do you serve at a celebration where the guest of honor isn’t a fan of cake or dessert? The Chronicle’s Food + Wine team briefly puzzled over this conundrum last year before alighting on the perfect party centerpiece: a whole muffuletta from Sandy’s. If you’re not feeding a crowd, the New Orleans sandwich, loaded with cured meats, provolone and piquant olive spread, is available in sensible slices as well.

name: Bombera
description: The lifeblood of Bombera, chef Dominica Rice-Cisneros’ vegetable-forward Mexican restaurant in Oakland, is its regiment of women. They are the tortillerias, the mole masters, the bartenders, the servers and the upholders of the cuisine’s traditions.

name: Verjus
description: Michael and Lindsay Tusk opened Verjus in 2019 as a casual bar and bottle shop with a natural wine bent. It closed during the pandemic, but was reborn in 2024 with a cocktail program and an expanded food menu. Above the open kitchen is a marquee whose warm glow reflects on the polished wood floors and candy-coated red ceiling that would make for a killer dance floor, all reverberating with a constant, high-energy din.

name: Californios
description: How lucky we are to live in the Bay Area during the era of Californios, a restaurant that pushes the scope of Mexican cuisine to dizzying heights. Corn is nixtamalized in house, as you might expect. But so are sweet potatoes and mandarins. The sake is Mexican. Ingredients for mole are presented on a platter like an abundant grazing board.

name: Rose Pizzeria
description: Rose Pizzeria defies categorization. The makeup of its pies shows traces of various pizza cultures: Chicago tavern-style crispness, New York-inspired airiness and Californian Neapolitan all inform the dough recipe, which has the depth of sourdough but without excessive chewiness. The result is lithe pies with crackly crusts and well-browned undercarriages.

name: Delfina
description: When Craig and Annie Stoll opened Delfina in 1998, it set a new bar for Italian cooking in the city. It nearly went away in 2020, when the Mission restaurant was on hiatus, only to be reborn two years later. The rejuvenated interior, which takes a cue from Italian architecture, now matches the eloquence of the menu, with a copper-colored metal mesh arch, burnished oak bar and two alcoves that house framed Photoshopped images of James Beard and Julia Child reimagined with tattoo sleeves.

name: Atelier Crenn
description: With everything Dominique Crenn has going on — opening one restaurant in Dior’s flagship store on Rodeo Drive, another in a new Waldorf Astoria property in Texas’ Hill Country — you’d be forgiven for assuming that Atelier Crenn is an afterthought, the taxidermic centerpiece in her trophy room. Not so.

name: Ok’s Deli
description: Ok’s Deli puts an Asian American spin on the deli format, localized to Oakland. The Town’s Filipino population is represented in the thunderously rich sisig bolio, heavy with sticky, gelatinous pork head, while the banh mi, slotted with lemongrass pork and slicked with pate, pays homage to the city’s many Vietnamese restaurants.

name: Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement
description: Chef-owner Fernay McPherson, a daughter of the Fillmore, has brought soul food back home. After years of operating a lauded food stall in Emeryville, McPherson opened Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement on Fillmore Street in 2024, and the national accolades soon followed. In front of nearly every diner at the no-frills restaurant is a pile of exemplary fried chicken, scented with just enough rosemary to make you sit up and take notice, and probably a side of peerless mac and cheese as well.

name: Hilda and Jesse
description: Fun is mandatory at Hilda and Jesse. The interior looks like something out of “Pee-wee’s Playhouse”: checkered floors, a wood squiggle running across the pastel cyan-and-white-striped wall and a colorful mural of women surrounded by plants that greets patrons.

name: Zuni Café
description: San Francisco is a city full of iconic restaurants and, while we love them for their atmosphere, history and sense of place, only a few still execute well enough to make this list. Chief among them is Zuni, which, for nearly half a century, has defended its title as the favorite restaurant of City Hall types, gays about town and your mother-in-law.

name: Madcap
full_address: 198 Sir Francis Drake Blvd., San Anselmo, CA
description: Your initial impression of this fine dining restaurant in San Anselmo might be that it’s a little too mad. The canvases that line the walls are as many as they are zany. Is that Barbarella standing atop a mountain of trash? Why is that woman wearing a tiny top hat pulling a lion out of a slightly larger top hat? But chef Ron Siegel’s tasting menu — American with French and Japanese touches and a relative bargain compared to others at $140 for eight courses on weeknights — is substance as well as style.

name: Lunette
full_address: 1 Ferry Building, Suite 33/47, San Francisco, CA
description: The Ferry Building is at its best when it reflects the homegrown talents of the Bay Area’s food scene, and Lunette, the Bay Area’s foremost Cambodian restaurant, is a prime example. Following the closure of Nyum Bai in Oakland in 2022, Nite Yun returned with a counter and small dining room graced by artist Nak Bou’s gorgeous mural that transports patrons to Phnom Penh’s Central Market.

name: State Bird Provisions
description: Are you the type of person who arrives at a restaurant with your exact order planned out, having pored over the menu online? If so, State Bird Provisions is not the restaurant for you. If, however, you find felicity in the unexpected and delight in the spontaneous, book a table at the Progress’ sister restaurant, which has served dim sum-style small plates since 2012.

name: Flour + Water
description: Flour + Water, one of the region’s defining Cal-Italian restaurants, remains fanatical about pasta, offering more than 80 shapes — all contained in an actual encyclopedia. For good reason, though, the most celebrated is the scarpinocc, a raft-shaped pasta with a dimple that serves as a reservoir for milky Taleggio cheese and tart balsamic vinegar.

name: La Vaca Birria
description: The single best burrito in the Bay Area comes from a halal Mexican restaurant. La Vaca Birria’s grilled cheese burrito is somehow greater than even the considerable sum of its Platonically ideal parts: a halfway gooey cheese skirt, guacamole, refried beans and flame-roasted fillings, all swaddled in a flour tortilla that’s toasted in beef tallow. Cut in half, it oozes mozzarella cheese and smells of sweet mesquite from the wood-burning grill.

name: Aziza
description: Until recently, if you Googled “Aziza San Francisco,” the first result was not the Moroccan restaurant’s website, but one operated by its most loyal customers. “One of the best restaurants in all of San Francisco remains closed at this time,” the site proclaims, “but as huge fans of Aziza, we are hopefully and patiently waiting its return.”

name: Bo Ne Phu Yen
description: Bells are constantly ringing at the Vietnamese food court at Lion Plaza in San Jose — the sound of kiosks alerting their patrons for pickup. The most popular one has no bell, but if you scan the angular dining room, nearly every table is topped by sizzling bó nè, steak and eggs on cast irons shaped like cows.

name: Fikscue
description: After running a home restaurant for two years, Reka and Fik Selah opened Fikscue in Alameda in 2023, combining Indonesian flavor with low-and-slow Texas barbecue to create the Bay Area’s greatest barbecue operation. Their two inspirations beautifully align in brisket rendang — smoked beef cubes stir-fried with coconut chile paste — and giant beef back ribs topped with tamarind-infused peanut sauce.

name: Lily
description: Lily is a date-night Vietnamese spot, with handsome latticed woodwork on the ceiling and a tufted leather banquette that runs the length of the narrow restaurant. But for those in the know, there are also deals to be had.

name: LeYou Ethiopian
full_address: 1100 N. First St., Suite C, San Jose, CA
description: LeYou in San Jose puts Ethiopian food in a new light, offering hard-to-find dishes while introducing novel takes on the cuisine. The latter is exemplified by its exceptional kitfo bites: tuna (or salmon) tartare marinated in awaze, a fiery oil laced with mitmita (the spicier cousin of berbere), accompanied by crispy injera strips with a Cheez-It-like sharpness; the vegan version ditches fish for a custardy eggplant dip complemented by tomatoes.

name: Mijoté
description: Here in San Francisco, the closest you’re going to get to the 11th arrondissement is a former sushi counter on a quiet corner in the Mission District. This is where Kosuke Tada serves a nightly set menu of four courses for $84, inspired by his years as head chef at bistro Le 6 Paul Bert in Paris.

name: Nari
description: There are two hotel restaurants on this list, and both of them belong to Thai restaurateur Pim Techamuanvivit, the recipient of last year’s inaugural Charles Phan Legacy Award. Nari is the fancier of the two spots, with monsteras and reclaimed teak softening the concrete elements of the dramatic dining room. The ambition of the menu is as lofty as the ceilings, with an emphasis on unexpected produce.

name: Tanzie’s
description: Tanzie’s is a double whammy: a Thai restaurant that is one of the best brunch spots in the Bay Area. Cute and welcoming, the interior is painted a radiant lime green, while a fireplace makes the space feel like the home of co-owners Krissana “Tanz” Tussanaprasit and Jezreel “Jae” Rojas. The couple met while working at Kin Khao before teaming up to create Tanzie’s, inspired by Tussanaprasit’s upbringing in Chiang Mai.

name: Mister Jiu’s
description: Brandon Jew’s landmark modern Chinese restaurant celebrates its 10th anniversary this year, and it does so triumphantly. Last year, Mister Jiu’s landed on the Top 100, but close readers might have noticed the suggestion to eat at the bar.

name: Yeobo, Darling
full_address: 827 Santa Cruz Ave., Menlo Park, CA
description: Menlo Park scores two new Top 100 restaurants this year — Eylan, at No. 70, and Yeobo, Darling, a Korean-Taiwanese restaurant from couple Meichih and Michael Kim. Locals may remember them from Maum, their intimate Michelin-starred tasting menu spot that closed in 2020. While Yeobo, Darling’s format is a la carte, the environs still scream fine dining, with silverware caddies built into the custom tables and the most comfortable chairs of any restaurant in the Bay Area.

name: Dalida
full_address: 101 Montgomery St., Suite 100, San Francisco, CA
description: Dalida was a great restaurant when it opened in the Presidio in 2023. Since then, it’s gotten even better, and it’s making its well-earned debut on this year’s Top 100. It bills itself as a Mediterranean restaurant, but think more Turkey and the Levant, less Spain and Italy.

name: La Taqueria
description: Now more than 50 years old, La Taqueria is a defining Mexican restaurant in San Francisco and home to perhaps the Bay Area’s most celebrated burrito, with lines of customers regularly curving around the block. Owner Miguel Jara, who grew up in Tijuana, hasn’t served rice in his restaurant since day one, and he never will.

name: Cotogna
description: Cotogna is Lindsay and Michael Tusk’s Goldilocks restaurant — far more affordable than Quince, far less rowdy than Verjus.

name: 3 Bottled Fish
description: Oakland’s Fruitvale neighborhood, which has a formidable street food culture, is made a more dynamic place to eat by 3 Bottled Fish, the Vietnamese café from Paulette Tran. Modestly sized, seating about a dozen patrons, the café has a homey decor, with plants, bookshelves and a TV showing footage of Vietnamese hawker stalls.

name: Prubechu
full_address: 2224 Mission St., A, San Francisco, CA
description: Chamorro food, the cuisine of Guam, may have limited options in the Bay Area, but Prubechu in San Francisco remains its strongest advocate. It’s impossible to nurse a bad mood seated in the cheery, plant-filled outdoor patio, where diners eat dry-style wings and barbecued chicken served over titiyas (leopard-spotted coconut flatbreads), meant to be eaten like a taco.

name: Valley
description: We are, for sure, like, totally Valley girls. The Sonoma restaurant/wine bar/café/bottle shop/mercantile is everything we want from a day-to-night establishment perched on a town square.

name: Kitchen Istanbul
description: Kitchen Istanbul landed on our list last year, but, if we’re being honest, it wasn’t exactly because of the food. Rather, the Inner Richmond gem was a favorite — of ours, as well as wine professionals and restaurant industry insiders — due to owner Emrah Kilicoglu’s knack for hospitality and the wine program overseen by Joseph DiGrigoli. Since last summer, however, Kitchen Istanbul’s contemporary Turkish menu has majorly leveled up under the leadership of Büşra Ayvaz.

name: Side A
description: It’s rare that a new restaurant feels like “Cheers” right out of the gate, but Side A radiated warmth from the moment it opened last spring. Chalk it up to owner Parker and Caroline Brown’s gregarious Midwestern hospitality, or the respectful design that built on the bones of the former inhabitant, neighborhood favorite Universal Café.

name: Liholiho Yacht Club
description: On an unusually cold San Francisco night, the “Aloha” spelled out in tiny hexagonal tile in Liholiho Yacht Club’s entryway feels like both a welcome and a promise. Chef Ravi Kapur hails from Oahu, and you’ll see Hawaii’s imprint in dishes like tuna poke, fried rice with housemade Spam and “Mom’s butter mochi.”

name: Kin Khao
full_address: 55 Cyril Magnin St., San Francisco, CA
description: Kin Khao is a trailblazer: At a time when San Francisco’s Thai food was dominated by pad Thai, chef Pim Techamuanvivit’s freshman effort shifted attention to Northern Thailand’s Issan cuisine. In the process, Kin Khao became the first Thai restaurant in California to hold a Michelin star — since 2015 — and left a lasting impression on the Bay Area.

name: Cultured Pickle Shop
description: Is Cultured Pickle Shop a restaurant? Does it speak to the best of Berkeley’s dining scene? Depends on who you ask. What’s not in question is that the fermentation emporium is home to one of the Bay Area’s most distinctive dishes, the extravagant rice bowl at the center of its weekends-only three-course menu, Rice & Pickles.

name: Lou’s Takeaway
full_address: 621A Del Ganado Road, San Rafael, CA
description: Lou’s Takeaway in San Rafael proposes that takeout doesn’t have to be a compromise of convenience, but undeniably delicious. Housed in a shack, the restaurant channels all its lifeforce into rice plates, which are informed by seasonal ingredients and the whims of chef Anthony “Lou” Rizzi, who has a penchant for Asian and Latino flavors.

name: The Happy Crane
description: San Francisco’s modern Chinese food scene is getting hotter and hotter, in no small part thanks to the Happy Crane, which opened last year in Hayes Valley. Chef-owner James Yeun Leong Parry cut his teeth at Michelin-starred restaurants in Hong Kong, London, Tokyo and San Francisco, then left it all behind to hone his dim sum skills at Palette Tea House in Ghirardelli Square.

name: Vik’s Chaat
description: For more than 36 years, Vik’s Chaat has been the standard bearer for Indian street food in the Bay Area. Based inside a Berkeley warehouse, Vik’s is half market and half cafeteria-style restaurant, with a spacious dining area surrounded by canary yellow walls.

name: Peña’s Bakery
description: The tortas at Peña’s Bakery in Oakland’s Fruitvale neighborhood make it worth getting out of bed early. Here, the barrio’s blue-collar workers line their bellies with pan dulce, coffee and weighty sandwiches stuffed with chorizo and potatoes. Made a day ahead to allow the potatoes to soak up the sausage fat, the boisterously rich filling is nestled in a fluffy, house-made telera bread, along with pepper strips and avocado.

name: Azalina’s
description: Azalina’s is the only restaurant in the Bay Area dedicated to Mamak cooking, the cuisine of Indian Muslims in Malaysia, and of chef Azalina Eusope’s family. The four-course tasting menu is her homage to them, a multigenerational lineage of street vendors whose cooking tools now sit on a shelf in the dining room. With each course, Eusope unravels a layer of her history.

name: Spicy Joi
description: Phengkhane “Joi” Simmaly, the charismatic chef-owner of Spicy Joi in Concord, is the Bay Area’s champion for Lao cuisine. He enthusiastically drops off trays, smiling and hamming it up as he lumbers under the weight of peppery sausages with taut skins, pork cracklings, smoky pepper relish and a pungent papaya salad.

name: Via Aurelia
full_address: 300 Toni Stone Xing, Suite A, San Francisco, CA
description: Via Aurelia, a Tuscan restaurant from the team behind Che Fico, anchors the building that houses the new Visa headquarters at Mission Rock, a slick live-work neighborhood just south of Oracle Park that opened last year.

name: Kunjip
description: The patrons of Kunjip, a staple of Santa Clara’s Koreatown since 2009, generally fall into two diametrically opposed categories: those seeking nourishment and those there for indulgence. The former group goes for the seolleongtang, a rejuvenating noodle soup with a cloudy oxtail bone broth; the latter gravitates toward the galbijjim, a stone pot filled with short ribs, inch-thick rice cakes and soft chunks of pumpkin in a sweet, fiery braising liquid at raging boil.

name: Cyrus
description: For all you fellow Augustus Gloops out there, if you want to view paradise, simply book a trip to Cyrus in Geyserville. Dinner ends with a visit to a hidden chamber that houses a chocolate waterfall, which smells as intoxicating as it sounds. If this seems like an atypical end to a fancy tasting menu, the dining experience at Cyrus isn’t exactly standard.

name: Psychic Pie
description: Every small town has a treasured local pizzeria, a spot in a strip mall where you can get an after-school slice and soft serve or a whole pie to-go on a Saturday night. Sebastopol’s happens to be a lot better than most.

name: Eylan
full_address: 500 El Camino Real, Menlo Park, CA
description: If two’s a coincidence and three’s a trend, then Eylan cements chef Srijith Gopinathan and restaurateur Ayesha Thapar as power players. Their third collaboration, following Ettan and Copra, opened early last year as the anchor of a suburban Menlo Park development that mixes condos with luxury watch retail.

name: Wolfsbane
description: When Rupert and Carrie Blease shuttered Lord Stanley last year, it brought to a close 10 years of Making It Work. During its decade in existence, the restaurant earned a Michelin star, provided city-subsidized meals to low-income residents during the pandemic, slung Filet-O-Fish-like sandwiches out of a takeout window and hosted chef residencies. With Wolfsbane, they’re attempting something simpler, sort of — an expensive tasting menu restaurant that aims to join the upper echelons of San Francisco fine dining.

name: Itria
description: If only every place serving a menu of pasta and crudos were as exceptional as Itria. The cozy Mission restaurant makes for a reliable neighborhood night-out spot — you can score a last-minute reservation, unlike at many of its Cal-Italian peers, and it doesn’t feel like a consolation.

name: Sirene
description: Sirene is the follow-up to Paul Einbund and Gavin Schmidt’s enduring San Francisco hit the Morris. Its idiosyncratic menu pairs fried chicken coated in a shatter-prone dredge with seafood like wood-fired mussels.

name: Rintaro
description: We are in an era of flavormaxxing, of restaurants that push the limits of salt, fat, acid and heat. And while that is a carnal pleasure, sometimes one wants … less. When what you crave is izakaya fare, simply and masterfully prepared, sink into a cushion of Rintaro’s silken tofu or a round of daikon gently simmered in dashi.

name: Table Culture Provisions
description: Table Culture Provisions is a fine dining restaurant full of loveable quirks. Unlike other restaurants with similar aspirations, it’s not located in a stately historic building or lavish new development. Instead, chef-owners Stéphane Saint Louis and Steven Vargas set up shop in a former diner by the name of Chili Joe’s.

name: Spread Kitchen
description: Lebanese cooking is presented in technicolor at Spread, a counter-service restaurant in Sonoma. Chef Cristina Topham incorporates lemons into nearly everything on the menu, especially the eponymous dish: the spreads, or mezzes.

name: Mustards Grill
description: To nostalgic locals, the 43-year-old Mustards Grill stands as a vestige of “Old Napa,” back when Wine Country still belonged to its residents. All the produce and wine is local, herbs are snipped from the garden out front, and even the buzz inside feels farm-fresh.

name: Scoma’s
full_address: 1965 Al Scoma Way, San Francisco, CA
description: Plans to overhaul Fisherman’s Wharf are underway, with the recent demolition of Alioto’s and several new restaurants opening this year. But one block over on Al Scoma Way, very little has changed since the ’70s. At Scoma’s, white-jacketed servers still rattle off the catch of the day — it’ll take a while — and warn you to remove your rings before getting intimate with the whole roasted Dungeness crab.

name: Jubba
description: Jubba, the Bay Area’s sole Somali restaurant, is another mark of San Jose as a singular food city. Located at a train station, the counter service restaurant is housed in a round building that could have been a carousel in a previous life. At one end is the register, at the other complimentary self-serve tea, which streams out hot, radiating with sugar and spice. The dining room fills with large parties, who congregate around the “sports plate,” a hulking tray of fatty goat ribs.

name: Smish Smash
description: Smish Smash makes the Bay Area’s quintessential smashburger. Founded by spouses Vic Donado and Amy Han in 2020, Smish began as a popup in the East Bay before landing at a kiosk at Saluhall, the IKEA-owned food hall in San Francisco. If it feels familiar, it’s partly because Donado has influenced many of his contemporaries to adopt his form and technique.

name: Reem’s
description: Reem’s is preparing to come home to Oakland after a five-year absence. A new bakery and café marks the next chapter for Reem Assil, who drew national attention for her first location in Fruitvale and its Californian takes on Arab street food.

name: Cafe Colucci
description: Cafe Colucci, one of Oakland’s pioneering Ethiopian restaurants, is at the top of its game. Founded by Fetlework Tefferi in 1991, initially as an Italian cafe, the restaurant operated in North Oakland for over 30 years before relocating to a building twice the size on the border of Oakland and Emeryville in 2022.

name: Saison
description: When Saison opened in 2013 after four years as a successful popup, its point of differentiation among other fine dining restaurants was that nearly everything was cooked over the fire, within view of customers — caveman cuisine, but soigné.

name: Enclos
description: Months before its opening, a Chronicle preview of Stone Edge Farm Estate Vineyards & Winery’s new restaurant Enclos teased, “Can this winery bring a Michelin star to Sonoma?” It did that and more, hopscotching straight to two stars — a coup for Sonoma, which has long been in the culinary shadow of neighboring Healdsburg.

name: The Laundromat
description: When pizza’s on a bagel, you can eat pizza anytime, and when a restaurant serves both pizzas and bagels, it will be busy all the time.

name: The Charter Oak
full_address: 1050 Charter Oak Ave., St. Helena, CA
description: Although it opened in 2018, the Charter Oak feels like it’s been a fixture for Napa Valley locals for well over a century — partly because its century-old stone facade remains intact. Revered by out-of-towners and locals, the live-fire American restaurant looks like a small castle made of brick, with a moody dining room that has a spark-sneezing hearth at its center.

name: Goodtime Bar
description: Part natural wine bar, part restaurant, Goodtime Bar is a rarity for San Jose, which suffers from a dearth of modern wine bars. Goodtime began as a spot primarily for pet nat enthusiasts, with a short list of snacks. But food has become integral to the operation, with chef and San Jose native Alex Whiteman, who cooked at Snail Bar in Oakland and Momofuku Noodle Bar in New York, delivering a menu that’s aggressively flavored but draws on the universal language of comfort food.

name: House of Prime Rib
full_address: 1906 Van Ness Ave., San Francisco, CA
description: House of Prime Rib attracts Bay Areans of all stripes, including native San Franciscans in Giants caps, suits and wraparound sunglasses and multigenerational Pinoy families celebrating birthdays. And when they walk through the door, they’re all King Henry VIII, entitled to “Hearty and Happily Generous” portions of beef, carved at stations set throughout the dining room.

name: Ernest
full_address: 1890 Bryant St., Suite 100, San Francisco, CA
description: Ernest’s initial success was a product of misfortune. One year after the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, few new restaurants were opening, but chef-owner Brandon Rice didn’t really have a choice — after months of costly delay, Ernest had to launch, even if he could only serve 10 outdoor tables a night.

name: Alem’s Coffee
description: Couple Alem Negash and Nigisity Eyasu opened Alem’s Coffee in 1999, and the Eritrean restaurant has been a hangout for East Africans seeking strong coffee and a fortifying breakfast ever since. The extraordinary shihan ful deserves to be canonized as a defining plate for Oakland.

name: Ippuku
description: One of the Bay Area’s preeminent yakitori joints, Ippuku regularly packs out with people having snacks at standing tables, eating grilled rice balls in booths and clinking sake glasses seated on tatami floor mats. UC Berkeley is within shouting distance, but the space, featuring woodwork by partner, Zen priest and masterbuilder Paul Discoe, feels remarkably far away.

name: Keeku da Dhaba
description: Varun Sapra mastered the flame at his food truck Keeku da Dhaba, which he operated for six years before settling into a permanent space in a Fremont plaza last year. He is usually out front tending to a two-tier charcoal grill, which he stokes with an industrial fan, adding rows of skewers filled with paneer, chicken thighs and ground lamb.

name: El Molino Central
description: El Molino Central, Sonoma’s greatest Mexican counter, gets deserved praise for its easygoing, lovely patio, a gravel lot with a covered pergola and a dozen turquoise picnic tables with red umbrellas. But the most telling thing about the restaurant is its name. The North Bay restaurant doubles as a molino, an old-school mill for nixtamalized corn, which yields fresh tortillas that are intoxicatingly fragrant, faintly sweet and soft with an eggy texture.

name: Mrs Khan Uyghur Cuisine
full_address: 712 Santa Cruz Ave., Menlo Park, CA
description: The reigning Uyghur restaurant in the Bay Area is in the sleepy suburb of Menlo Park. Opened in 2022, Mrs Khan specializes in hand-pulled noodles, which range from thick ropes to flat strings. The former stars in the splendid korma, an imposing platter of chewy noodles, peppers and well-browned chicken — the kind of food you’d eat in preparation for a snowstorm.

name: Mashaallah Halal Pakistani Food
description: There aren’t a lot of success stories surrounding the abandoned San Francisco Centre Mall — in fact, there may only be one. Mashaallah opened in the basement food court in 2022, past the Sunglasses Hut and Wetzel’s Pretzels. Mohammad and Rabia Waqar soon made a name for themselves among downtown office workers, serving grilled-to-order lamb chops alongside steam tray combo platters and strong cups of chai.

name: Patio Filipino
full_address: 1770 El Camino Real, San Bruno, CA
description: At Patio Filipino, a steaming bowl of sinigang, puckery with tamarind and packed with veggies and pork, could easily feed a family of six. That checks out: Large parties are the norm, with lolos and lolas bouncing babies on their knees while platters of peanutty oxtail stew and sizzling sisig circulate.

name: Capital
description: Capital opened in Chinatown in the 1980s, but credit for its current relevance goes to owner Samantha Lo, who bought the Cantonese restaurant in 2007. Lo introduced to the 100-item menu what is now the restaurant’s signature: the unbelievably crispy salt-and-pepper wings, each earth-shattering bite audible from San Jose.

name: Yo También Cantina
description: The tamal bowl from Yo También Cantina is the antidote to depressing slop bowls. Opened by Kenzie Benesh and Isabella Bertorelli in 2018 at the eastern edge of the Inner Sunset, the daytime restaurant has verve, an ideal spot for loitering in its parklet while sipping on a nonalcoholic “wellness” michelada, whose recipe calls for sparkling water, fruit puree and a chamoy rim.

name: Shizen
description: Shizen miraculously proves that great sushi is not incumbent on seafood — the entire menu is vegan. Established in 2015, the energetic San Francisco plant-based sushi bar and izakaya eschews the solemnity that’s de rigueur at many sushi spots, leaving more room for fun: The “Surprise Ending” consists of six rolls filled with avocado, asparagus and shredded tofu, then capped in beet slices; one contains a hot pepper, like playing sushi Russian roulette.

name: Lazy Bear
description: When you enter Lazy Bear, you leave the Mission and enter a luxurious forest. Diners assume the role of junior scouts, each given a pencil and a menu booklet, which includes space for field notes. Lazy Bear, an anagram of chef-owner David Barzelay’s surname, started as an underground dinner party at the chef’s apartment before opening as a restaurant in 2014. In its early years, diners were seated all at once, at long communal banquet tables. Today, book a table in the upstairs mezzanine level, furnished with leather couches and decorated with woodsy memorabilia.
